                      EMPLOYMENT
                      AND EARNINGS
                                                             VOL.1B NO.4 OCTOBER 1 9 7 2

                      Joseph M. Finerty, Editor
                      Gloria P. Green, Associate Editor
                      James A. McCail, Associate Editor



                                Editors' Note

                                    Beginning with this issue, ail national employment series in the establishment "Survey have been ad-
                                justed to March 1971 benchmarks. Hours, earnings, job vacancies, and labor turnover data may also
                                have been revised if there were any sizeable changes in the employment weights. The article, "BLS
                                Establishment Estimates Revised to March 1971 Benchmark Levels" (pages 5-15), contains a detailed
                                discussion of these revisions. New seasonal factors for current adjustment may be found on pages
                                 160-162. Data in table C-10 have not yet been adjusted to reflect the effects of the new benchmarks.
                                    The historical compendium, Employment and Earnings, United States, 1909-72, BLS Bulletin
                                 1312-9 to be released later will contain historical data adjusted to March 1971 benchmarks.

                                    October 1972 issue marks the introduction of March 1971 benchmarks.

                                by more than 300,000 as a result of the census adjustment. For an explanation of the changes and an indication of the dif-
                                ferences, see "Revisions in the Current Population Survey" in the February 1972 issue of Employment and Earnings.

                                                                                                      Seasonal Factors
                       The following tables present seasonal adjustment factors for all series in the establishment section of this periodical, which have been revised, as.in the past, coincidental with the adjustment of
                    the industry employment series to new benchmarks. These factors were derived using data through June 1972. The factors should be used with current estimates (adjusted to the March 1971 bench-
                    marks) for July 1972 through August 1973 or until the date of the next benchmark revision.
                       The seasonal movements are measured in order to adjust the data statistically for such recurring events as warm and cold weather, crop-growing cycles, holidays, vacations, regular industry model
                    changeover periods, and the like. These movements are generally the largest single component of month-to-month changes in employment, hours, earnings, job vacancies, and labor turnover. The
                    seasonal factors which follow enable the analyst to remove these influences from the data in order to determine more basic trends.

                     Job vacancies are the stock of unfilled job openings as of the
                 close of the last business day of the reference month. Openings
                 for all kinds of positions, classifications and employment, full
                                                                                            Size and regional stratification
                time, part time, permanent, temporary, and seasonal are in-
                cluded. Excluded are jobs to be filled by recall from layoff,                   A number of industries are stratified by size of establish-

                leave; jobs unoccupied because of labor-management disputes;                supervisory-worker data are used to weight the hours and earn-
                job openings for which "new" workers were already hired and                 ings into broader industry groupings. Accordingly, the basic esti-
                scheduled to start work later; and openings with future starting            mating cell for an employment, hours, or earnings series, as the
                dates, which are requested as a separate item.                              term is used in the summary of computational methods, may be
                    Job vacancies are defined as vacant jobs which are immedi-              a whole industry or a size stratum, a region stratum, or a size
                ately available for filling, and for which the firm is actively try-        stratum of a region within an industry.
                ing to find or recruit workers from outside the firm.

                                                                                                The estimates relating to the benchmark month are com-
                current job vacancies by the sum of employment plus vacancies,
                                                                                            pared with new benchmark levels, industry by industry. If re-
                and multiplying that quotient by 100.

                     Occupational classifications are made in accordance with
                                                                                            justed between the new benchmark and the preceding one, and
                those established in the Dictionary of Occupational Titles, Third
                                                                                            the new benchmark for each industry is then carried forward
                 Edition, U.S. Department of Labor, 1965.
                                                                                            progressively to the current month by use of the sample trends.

                                                                                             month-to-month changes in the level. A comparison of the actual
                    The principal features of the procedure used to estimate em-            amounts of revisions made in the last 3 benchmark years is
                ployment for the industry statistics are (1) the use of the "link           shown in table E.
                relative" technique, which is a form of ratio estimation, (2) pe-
                riodic adjustment of employment levels to new benchmarks, and
                (3) the use of size and regional stratification.

                 The "link relative" technique
                    From a sample composed of establishments reporting for                     Industry division        1969          1970          1971
                both the previous and current months, the ratio of current

                                                     Unemployment Insurance Data

                    Insured unemployment represents the number of persons               as a percent of average covered employment in a 12-month
                reporting a week of unemployment under an unemployment in-              period ending 6 to 8 months prior to the week of reference.
                surance program. It includes some persons who are working part          Initial claims are notices filed by those losing jobs covered by an
                time who would be counted as employed in the payroll and                unemployment insurance program that they are starting a
                household surveys. Excluded are persons who have exhausted              period of unemployment. A claimant who continued to be
                their benefit rights and workers who have not earned rights to          unemployed a full week is then counted in the insured un-
                unemployment insurance. In general, excluded from coverage              employment figure.
                are those persons engaged in'agriculture, domestic service, un-
                paid family work, selected nonprofit organizations, State and               Because of differences in State laws and procedures under
                local government and self-employment. Also excluded from                which unemployment insurance programs are operated, State un-
                the insured unemployment count, but included as employed in             employment rates generally indicate, but do not precisely mea-
                the household survey, are those persons who earned no wages             ure, differences among the individual States. Persons wishing to
                during the payroll period because they were temporarily absent          receive a detailed description of the nature, sources, inclusions
